evals.name: scroll-story-from-design-doc
evals.prompt: I've got a small event-sourcing order service. Orders come in over Kafka, an append-only event log is the source of truth, a pure fold derives the current order state into Postgres, and a projector publishes status events out. Build me an impressive animated intro page that tells this story as the user scrolls — I want to show it to non-technical stakeholders so they 'feel' how it works. Make it look genuinely professional, not like a generic AI page. Serve it locally and tell me how to open it.
evals.expected_output: A working scroll-story landing page: a fixed Three.js low-poly world the camera flies through on scroll (GSAP ScrollTrigger), a hero that tilts away, a request/order travelling the scene, sections revealing over it (Kafka in -> event log -> fold -> Postgres -> projector out). Restrained, professional styling (one accent, neutral greys, real type scale, no slop). Components carry data-viz-id; edit-mode is wired. Verified in a browser with a clean console.

assertions.text: Produces a runnable page (index.html + JS) that loads without errors
Uses Three.js for a 3D scene AND GSAP ScrollTrigger for scroll-driven animation
The 3D scene is a fixed/pinned stage the camera moves through on scroll (not a static canvas)
Styling shows restraint: a single reserved accent colour with neutral greys, not rainbow/gradient slop
Components carry data-viz-id attributes (the AI-tweakable convention)
Edit-mode is wired into the page (edit-mode.js/.css included)
The narrative maps to the described system (Kafka in -> event log -> fold -> Postgres -> projector out)

evals.name: architecture-map-with-lenses-and-erd
evals.prompt: Build an interactive architecture map page for our payments platform. The pieces: an API gateway, a payments service (NestJS), a ledger service, a fraud service, a Kafka bus between them, a Postgres ledger DB, and an external acquirer we call out to. I want switchable overlay 'lenses' (at least: Systems, Data Flow, Reliability), clickable service nodes that open a component drill-down, and a clean ERD for the ledger data model (accounts, transactions, entries with proper crow's-foot relationships). Dark 'blueprint' look. Make it pan/zoomable and let me leave visual feedback on it.
evals.expected_output: A dark blueprint architecture map: an SVG system map with the named nodes in region lanes, an overlay-lens switcher (Systems/Data Flow/Reliability) that dims non-active aspects, clickable nodes opening a drill-down component diagram, and a separate ERD with crow's-foot notation (cardinality at endpoints, lines that don't cross tables, hub-and-spoke layout). Pan/zoom on the diagrams. Edit-mode wired. data-viz-ids present. Verified in a browser, clean console.

assertions.text: Produces a runnable architecture-map page that loads without console errors
Has an overlay-lens switcher with at least Systems / Data Flow / Reliability that changes what the map emphasises
The named nodes (gateway, payments, ledger, fraud, Kafka, Postgres, acquirer) all appear in the map
Service nodes are clickable and open a component drill-down view
Includes an ERD with crow's-foot notation: cardinality shown at endpoints and lines that do not cross tables
Dark blueprint aesthetic (dark bg, thin grid, teal/amber semantic accents), data-viz-ids present, edit-mode wired

evals.name: edit-feedback-revision-loop
evals.prompt:
Here's a batch of visual feedback from the edit-mode tool on the architecture page you built:

## 1. component: hero.title
This is too big and a bit shouty — make it ~25% smaller and tighten the line-height.

## 2. area snip — components inside: arch.node.ledger, arch.node.fraud
These two nodes sit too close together, it looks cramped. Add clear horizontal space between them.

## 3. object: scene.gateway
The gateway object in the 3D scene is too small and easy to miss — make it noticeably larger and give it the brand accent colour so it reads as the entry point.

Apply all three.
evals.expected_output: Each feedback item is resolved by mapping its stable id (hero.title / the two arch.node ids / scene.gateway) to the exact builder/CSS rule and making the change — without asking which element the user meant. The model demonstrates the one-hop id->code workflow: shrink the hero title via its data-viz-id rule, space the two named nodes, enlarge + recolor the registered 3D object by its userData.vizId. Changes verified in the browser.

assertions.text: Resolves each feedback item by its stable id without asking the user which element they meant
hero.title is made smaller with tighter line-height (mapped via its data-viz-id rule)
The two named arch.node ids are given more horizontal spacing between them
The 3D object scene.gateway is enlarged AND recoloured to the accent, found via userData.vizId
Demonstrates the one-hop id->builder workflow rather than guessing or broad rewrites
